artifacts.description: Associative learning in striatum is controlled by a three-factor interaction: (i) synapse-specific eligibility generated by recent glutamatergic input and postsynaptic activation; (ii) a phasic dopamine reward prediction error (RPE) that converts eligibility into durable synaptic change; and (iii) intracellular biochemical state (cAMP/PKA/DARPP-32/CaMKII, PDE regulation) that imposes strong tempor
artifacts.name: Eligibility-Window Gain Theory (EWGT) — revised

data.name: Eligibility-Window Gain Theory (EWGT) — revised
data.description: Associative learning in striatum is controlled by a three-factor interaction: (i) synapse-specific eligibility generated by recent glutamatergic input and postsynaptic activation; (ii) a phasic dopamine reward prediction error (RPE) that converts eligibility into durable synaptic change; and (iii) intracellular biochemical state (cAMP/PKA/DARPP-32/CaMKII, PDE regulation) that imposes strong temporal gating and nonlinear amplitude sensitivity. Under EWGT, increasing |RPE| increases the *effective learning rate* only when dopamine arrives within a permissive eligibility window and when intracellular pathways are in a permissive regime; outside those conditions, the learning rate is near-zero regardless of RPE magnitude. This explains why simply making dopamine larger (or more global) can fail to speed learning, and why timing/space/cell-type context can dominate the magnitude–learning relationship.

content.title: Multiplicative three-factor gain rule for striatal learning rate

content.text: At corticostriatal synapses, incremental associative learning per event follows a multiplicative three-factor rule: Δw ∝ E_pre/post(t) · G_DA(RPE(t), local_DA(t)) · S_cell(state), where E_pre/post is an eligibility trace from recent glutamatergic input and postsynaptic activation, G_DA is a dopamine/RPE-dependent gain term, and S_cell captures cell-type/biochemical-state gating (e.g., D1 vs D2, cAMP/PDE constraints).
**Supporting evidence:**

- Single-spine STDP-like activation paired with phasic optogenetic DA causes robust spine enlargement and increased 2pEPSC, indicating DA acts on a pre-existing eligibility trace rather than acting as a standalone instructive signal.  <paper entityId="paper-88ecdedd5defe65dd4eda45aa802636be71322d6" annotationId="ann_001" corpusId="206558487" artifactId="extraction-result-6#uuid-e6.0">Yagishita et al., 2014</paper>

- Review-level statement that regulated dopamine release accompanying activity at relevant corticostriatal synapses is essential to reinforce relevant motor acts, consistent with a three-factor (activity × DA) mechanism.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_002" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.3">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

- cAMP levels interact with afferent glutamatergic activity to govern bidirectional plasticity in striatopallidal (D2) neurons, consistent with dopamine-modulated intracellular state multiplying afferent-driven eligibility.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_003" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.6">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

- A cAMP pathway is hypothesized to mediate consolidation of learning after positive vs negative RPE by elevating cAMP in D1 vs D2 neurons respectively, i.e., DA/RPE controls whether eligibility becomes consolidated.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_004" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.5">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

- Diffuse-DA RFLO model implements explicit three-factor learning: corticostriatal update dw ∝ eligibility p_ji × dopamine-derived neuron-specific factor γ_j; learning speed depends on this multiplicative structure.  <paper entityId="paper-ce31f95044d791f74aebf950ee5f5a2e273e8090" annotationId="ann_005" corpusId="260377246" artifactId="extraction-result-1#uuid-e1.0">Wärnberg et al., 2023</paper>

- Spiking BCPNN basal ganglia model implements a four-factor rule with an RPE-dependent learning-rate multiplier κ scaling synaptic P-trace updates, formalizing DA/RPE as multiplicative gain on plasticity.  <paper entityId="paper-54c83edfdc7c06e3bf7e7d6f9fad7c8ed0d74c3b" annotationId="ann_006" corpusId="14681021" artifactId="extraction-result-2#uuid-e2.0">Berthet et al., 2016</paper>

- Optogenetic studies show DA transients timed to prediction-error moments are sufficient/necessary for acquisition of associations, consistent with DA multiplying eligibility created by cue transitions/outcome moments.  <paper entityId="paper-960aedce3b5f378a1aea1f7bbae9c17634d8d313" annotationId="ann_007" corpusId="238223285" artifactId="extraction-result-5#uuid-e5.0">Millard et al., 2021</paper>

- Genetic disruption of DA-neuron bursting (reducing phasic DA) impairs aversive conditioning, consistent with loss of the gain term (G_DA) preventing eligibility-to-learning conversion.  <paper entityId="paper-8c1f212c551ca7dd0d260aea0d14bb2a434c2ea1" annotationId="ann_008" corpusId="9672349" artifactId="extraction-result-3#uuid-e3.3">Lloyd et al., 2016</paper>

- In PD models/review, motor experience during DA deficiency produces aberrant inhibitory learning, consistent with DA-gated consolidation mechanisms interacting with experience (eligibility) to shape effective learning rate over repetitions.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_009" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.7">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

content.title: Temporal-window law: RPE magnitude has effect only within an eligibility-constrained delay range

content.text: For a fixed eligibility induction protocol, the sensitivity of learning to |RPE| is strongly time-gated: ∂(Δw)/∂|RPE| > 0 only when dopamine arrives within a limited post-eligibility delay window; outside this window, ∂(Δw)/∂|RPE| ≈ 0 (increasing |RPE| cannot rescue plasticity when mistimed).
**Supporting evidence:**

- DA promoted D1R-MSN spine enlargement only within ~0.3–2 s after glutamatergic inputs (peak ~0.6 s); mis-timed DA (e.g., 5 s delay) was ineffective, demonstrating a window-shaped dependence.  <paper entityId="paper-88ecdedd5defe65dd4eda45aa802636be71322d6" annotationId="ann_010" corpusId="206558487" artifactId="extraction-result-6#uuid-e6.0">Yagishita et al., 2014</paper>

- PDE10A inhibition (papaverine) abolished spatial suppression and altered the timing/compartment constraints on PKA activation, indicating biochemical control of the effective timing window.  <paper entityId="paper-88ecdedd5defe65dd4eda45aa802636be71322d6" annotationId="ann_011" corpusId="206558487" artifactId="extraction-result-6#uuid-e6.0">Yagishita et al., 2014</paper>

- Review statement: timing of regulated dopamine release accompanying activity at relevant corticostriatal synapses is essential for reinforcing relevant acts—supporting timing as a necessary condition, not just magnitude.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_012" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.3">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

content.title: Biochemical gate & nonlinearity law: RPE magnitude maps to learning via thresholded, pathway-specific intracellular cascades

content.text: The function mapping RPE magnitude to plasticity/learning rate is nonlinear and pathway-specific because dopamine modulates intracellular cAMP/PKA/DARPP-32 (and related phosphatase/kinase balance) that gates CaMKII-dependent potentiation (and bidirectional plasticity in D2 circuits). Consequently, small |RPE| can produce negligible learning below an intracellular threshold, moderate |RPE| produces strong learning, and large |RPE| yields diminishing returns once downstream targets saturate or consolidation limits are reached.
**Supporting evidence:**

- D1R antagonist (SCH23390), PKA inhibitor (PKI), DARPP-32 inhibitory peptide, and AC1 blocker (NB001) abolish DA-gated spine enlargement and PKA activation, demonstrating a biochemical gate rather than a simple linear DA-amplitude effect.  <paper entityId="paper-88ecdedd5defe65dd4eda45aa802636be71322d6" annotationId="ann_013" corpusId="206558487" artifactId="extraction-result-6#uuid-e6.0">Yagishita et al., 2014</paper>

- Strong correlation between PKA (AKAR2) activation magnitude and spine volume change across DA timing conditions (Spearman rho≈0.94) supports an intracellular gain variable controlling plasticity magnitude.  <paper entityId="paper-88ecdedd5defe65dd4eda45aa802636be71322d6" annotationId="ann_014" corpusId="206558487" artifactId="extraction-result-6#uuid-e6.0">Yagishita et al., 2014</paper>

- cAMP and afferent activity jointly determine LTP vs LTD in D2 MSNs, implying intracellular threshold/gating control (not linear scaling) over plasticity direction and magnitude in the indirect pathway.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_015" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.6">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

- cAMP-based consolidation hypothesis explicitly links RPE sign to cAMP elevation in D1 vs D2 neurons and to protein-synthesis-dependent consolidation, implying nonlinear thresholds for durable learning.  <paper entityId="paper-991ab459898dc4202ac3f306dd997b2c0ef060ca" annotationId="ann_016" corpusId="277674626" artifactId="extraction-result-4#uuid-e4.5">Shi et al., 2025</paper>

- Spiking BCPNN model implements a nonlinear (power-law) mapping from dopaminergic activity to learning-rate multiplier κ; as RP prediction improves, RPE and weight-change amplitude decrease, illustrating nonlinear dependence of learning rate on error magnitude.  <paper entityId="paper-54c83edfdc7c06e3bf7e7d6f9fad7c8ed0d74c3b" annotationId="ann_017" corpusId="14681021" artifactId="extraction-result-2#uuid-e2.0">Berthet et al., 2016</paper>

- PD-like DA-neuron deletions in the spiking model shift tonic/phasic DA, producing impaired learning, consistent with baseline-dependent threshold shifts in the DA→plasticity mapping.  <paper entityId="paper-54c83edfdc7c06e3bf7e7d6f9fad7c8ed0d74c3b" annotationId="ann_018" corpusId="14681021" artifactId="extraction-result-2#uuid-e2.0">Berthet et al., 2016</paper>

- Ex vivo (NAc core D1R-MSN) dose–response: increasing optogenetic DA burst strength/duration will increase spine enlargement only when DA is delivered within ~0.3–2 s after STDP induction; outside this window the slope of enlargement vs DA magnitude will be ~0.
- In vivo Pavlovian conditioning: delivering optogenetic DA bursts at an outcome time that is systematically shifted outside the animal’s typical eligibility window (e.g., several seconds late) will fail to accelerate acquisition despite large DA transients, whereas smaller properly timed bursts will accelerate acquisition.
- PDE10A inhibition will broaden the behavioral delay tolerance for reinforcement timing (larger set of outcome delays still produce learning), but will also reduce synaptic/credit specificity (more widespread potentiation).

- Across striatal territories, the optimal DA–eligibility delay for maximal learning-rate sensitivity to |RPE| will covary with local PDE expression and dendritic geometry; mapping these will predict region-specific ‘eligibility half-lives’.
- In tasks with jittered outcome timing, animals will compensate by altering striatal DA wave propagation timing (or local DA kinetics) to keep DA arrival within the eligibility window; experimentally forcing a mismatch will selectively reduce learning-rate sensitivity to |RPE|.
- If ensemble/content-coded DA errors (sensory prediction errors) are delivered with identical bulk amplitude but different patterns, synapse-specific learning will differ even when timing is identical—implying dissociation between bulk DA amplitude and effective associative learning rate.

- Show robust distal-spine potentiation in D1R-MSNs with DA delivered far outside the reported window (>5–10 s) while keeping the same eligibility induction; this would undermine strong window gating.
- Show that blocking D1R/PKA/DARPP-32/AC1 does not reduce DA-gated potentiation and that higher DA magnitude fully rescues it; this would contradict biochemical gating.
- Demonstrate in vivo that learning rate increases monotonically with DA transient amplitude even when DA timing is decorrelated from cue/action/outcome timing (randomized delays), implying magnitude-only control.

- Region- and context-dependent sign reversals of DA transients to aversive cues (e.g., NAc shell increases vs core decreases) indicate that ‘RPE magnitude’ is not a uniform scalar across striatum; EWGT does not by itself explain which circuits get positive vs negative teaching signals.  <paper entityId="paper-8c1f212c551ca7dd0d260aea0d14bb2a434c2ea1" annotationId="ann_019" corpusId="9672349" artifactId="extraction-result-3#uuid-e3.2">Lloyd et al., 2016</paper>

- Evidence that DA neuron ensembles carry sensory/content information about prediction errors suggests learning depends on error *content* as well as magnitude/timing; EWGT does not specify how content is routed to particular synapses beyond generic eligibility.  <paper entityId="paper-960aedce3b5f378a1aea1f7bbae9c17634d8d313" annotationId="ann_020" corpusId="238223285" artifactId="extraction-result-5#uuid-e5.8">Millard et al., 2021</paper>

- Human striatal DA signals can encode superposed actual and counterfactual errors, complicating any direct mapping from a single measured DA amplitude to a single learning-rate effect.  <paper entityId="paper-8c1f212c551ca7dd0d260aea0d14bb2a434c2ea1" annotationId="ann_021" corpusId="9672349" artifactId="extraction-result-3#uuid-e3.5">Lloyd et al., 2016</paper>

artifacts.description: The impact of dopamine RPE magnitude on striatal learning rate is determined not only by how large the RPE is, but by how *informationally structured* the dopaminergic signal is across space, time, and ensemble identity. Learning is fastest when each striatal microdomain receives a distinct, approximately independent projection of the multidimensional error relevant to that domain (vector-valued,
artifacts.name: Distributed Error Projection Theory (DEPT)

data.name: Distributed Error Projection Theory (DEPT)
data.description: The impact of dopamine RPE magnitude on striatal learning rate is determined not only by how large the RPE is, but by how *informationally structured* the dopaminergic signal is across space, time, and ensemble identity. Learning is fastest when each striatal microdomain receives a distinct, approximately independent projection of the multidimensional error relevant to that domain (vector-valued, heterogeneous dopamine), enabling effective credit assignment. In contrast, a large but homogeneous/global dopamine signal can carry high magnitude yet low instructional rank, yielding little improvement in associative learning rate for complex behaviors.

content.title: Instructional-rank law: learning speed scales with the effective dimensionality of DA error projections, not bulk amplitude

content.text: For tasks requiring learning of multidimensional mappings/policies, the rate of corticostriatal associative learning increases with the effective rank/independence of dopamine error projections across SPNs (diverse local DA concentrations reflecting different error components), and can be low even at high |RPE| when dopamine is spatially homogeneous (low-rank teaching signal).
**Supporting evidence:**

- Diffuse-DA RFLO model: heterogeneous, neuron-specific dopamine feedback improves learning speed and final loss; performance exhibits a 'sweet spot' at intermediate dopamine spatial spread λ; extremes (too local or too global/homogeneous) impair learning.  <paper entityId="paper-ce31f95044d791f74aebf950ee5f5a2e273e8090" annotationId="ann_001" corpusId="260377246" artifactId="extraction-result-1#uuid-e1.0">Wärnberg et al., 2023</paper>

- Homogeneous scalar dopamine control performs no better than readout-only learning, demonstrating that global amplitude without heterogeneity can fail to increase learning rate.  <paper entityId="paper-ce31f95044d791f74aebf950ee5f5a2e273e8090" annotationId="ann_002" corpusId="260377246" artifactId="extraction-result-1#uuid-e1.1">Wärnberg et al., 2023</paper>

- Empirical recordings show spatiotemporal heterogeneity (waves) and regional differences in striatal DA signals, consistent with the need for structured, localized teaching signals rather than a single global scalar.  <paper entityId="paper-960aedce3b5f378a1aea1f7bbae9c17634d8d313" annotationId="ann_003" corpusId="238223285" artifactId="extraction-result-5#uuid-e5.3">Millard et al., 2021</paper>

- Primary reward vs reward-predictive cues evoke different phasic DA patterns across striatal sites, indicating that instructional signals differ by location and event type.  <paper entityId="paper-960aedce3b5f378a1aea1f7bbae9c17634d8d313" annotationId="ann_004" corpusId="238223285" artifactId="extraction-result-5#uuid-e5.7">Millard et al., 2021</paper>

content.title: Optimal-mixing law: learning is maximized at intermediate DA diffusion/propagation scales

content.text: The dependence of learning rate on dopaminergic spread is nonmonotonic: intermediate spatial mixing (neither too local nor too global) maximizes learning by ensuring both coverage and non-degenerate, neuron-specific error mixtures; overly slow temporal filtering of DA reduces learning unless compensated by synaptic temporal filtering.
**Supporting evidence:**

- Diffuse-DA RFLO model shows inverted-U dependence on spatial spread λ ('sweet spot') and monotonic deterioration with increasing DA temporal filtering τ_DA unless synaptic high-pass compensation exists.  <paper entityId="paper-ce31f95044d791f74aebf950ee5f5a2e273e8090" annotationId="ann_005" corpusId="260377246" artifactId="extraction-result-1#uuid-e1.0">Wärnberg et al., 2023</paper>

- Wave-like DA dynamics in dorsal striatum suggest task-dependent propagation patterns that could tune spatiotemporal spread for credit assignment, consistent with an optimal spread principle.  <paper entityId="paper-960aedce3b5f378a1aea1f7bbae9c17634d8d313" annotationId="ann_006" corpusId="238223285" artifactId="extraction-result-5#uuid-e5.3">Millard et al., 2021</paper>

- If DA spread is experimentally broadened (e.g., by uptake inhibition) in a task requiring fine credit assignment, learning speed will initially improve from too-local conditions but will worsen past an intermediate optimum as DA becomes too homogeneous across microdomains.
- In tasks with multidimensional continuous control, manipulations that increase heterogeneity of DA projections (e.g., stimulating spatially distinct DA axon subsets conveying different error components) will increase learning rate more than equal-amplitude global stimulation.
- Bulk DA amplitude measured in one striatal site will be a weaker predictor of learning speed than a multichannel measure capturing spatial patterning (e.g., multi-fiber DA recordings).

- A measurable 'teaching-signal rank' (e.g., from simultaneous DA recordings across sites) will quantitatively predict trial-by-trial learning-rate variability better than average DA amplitude across the striatum.
- DA wave directionality will adapt when task-relevant error dimensions change; disrupting wave direction (without changing amplitude) will slow learning.
- In human striatum, counterfactual-error components will selectively modulate learning only in striatal subregions receiving high-rank (less mixed) projections, predicting patient- and site-specific learning-rate effects.

- Show that homogeneous/global DA stimulation (matched in total DA release) accelerates high-dimensional learning as much as heterogeneous stimulation; this would refute the instructional-rank dependence.
- Show that varying DA diffusion/uptake monotonically improves learning (no intermediate optimum) across tasks that require credit assignment, contradicting the optimal-mixing law.
- Show that DA spatial patterning does not correlate with learning-rate variability when controlling for mean amplitude, contradicting DEPT’s central claim.

- Single-spine biochemical timing windows (D1R-MSN, PDE10A/AC1 dependence) specify micro-level gating; DEPT is primarily about distributed error structure and does not specify these intracellular constraints.  <paper entityId="paper-88ecdedd5defe65dd4eda45aa802636be71322d6" annotationId="ann_007" corpusId="206558487" artifactId="extraction-result-6#uuid-e6.0">Yagishita et al., 2014</paper>

metadata.title: Feasibility of dopamine as a vector-valued feedback signal in the basal ganglia
metadata.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

tldr.text: The feasibility of multidimensional feedback using dopamine in the basal ganglia is demonstrated and testable predictions are made to improve learning compared to a single signal, despite the nonspecificity of the projections.

journal.name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

metadata.abstract: Significance The striatum is thought to learn to select actions based on environmental feedback and rewards using a dopamine feedback signal. When the action is continuous and mutlidimensional, e.g., a reaching movement, it is beneficial that the dopamine feedback has the same dimensionality as the task. However, although the dopaminergic cell bodies exhibit multidimensional responses, it has been unclear whether they can be effectively used by the striatum given their broad and unspecific axonal arbors. We present a simplified simulation model where multidimensional dopamine activity improves learning compared to a single signal, despite the nonspecificity of the projections. Thus, we demonstrate the feasibility of multidimensional feedback using dopamine in the basal ganglia and make testable predictions.

content.title: Diffuse dopamine, vector-valued reward-prediction-error implemented with RFLO learning in a basal-ganglia-like recurrent network

| name_short | Diffuse-DA RFLO model |
| name_full | Diffuse dopamine, vector-valued reward-prediction-error implemented with RFLO learning in a basal-ganglia-like recurrent network |
| brief_description | A computational recurrent network model of a striatal microcircuit that implements RFLO three-factor plasticity where SNc units encode a vector-valued error (RPE) whose varicosity-based diffusion to SPNs produces neuron-specific dopamine concentrations that drive corticostriatal and striatofugal learning; used to relate RPE magnitude/structure to speed and accuracy of learning continuous multidimensional motor outputs. |
| citation_title | here |
| mention_or_use | use |
| species_and_subjects | No biological subjects — in silico computational model / rate-based recurrent neural network (simulated populations representing cortex, thalamus, striatum (SPNs), SNr/GPi readout, and SNc dopaminergic units). Results are medians over 25 simulation runs for each condition. |
| behavioral_paradigm | Simulated associative motor learning: network learns to produce repeated d-dimensional continuous target trajectories (here d=4) over 200 ms trials (T=200 ms) drawn from a Gaussian process; task is function-approximation of continuous trajectories (analogous to skilled reaching) — acquisition learning across repeated trial presentations (5,000 trials), not a discrete Pavlovian/operant protocol with explicit cue-outcome probabilities. |
| striatal_region_and_circuit | Abstracted dorsal striatum (SPNs) projecting to a readout population identified as GPi/SNr; midbrain dopaminergic projection modeled as SNc providing nigrostriatal feedback; model includes direct (dSPN) and indirect (iSPN) pathways and plastic striatofugal (striatum→SNr/GPi) projections. |
| cell_type_and_receptors | Modelled cell types: striatal projection neurons (SPNs) separated into direct-pathway (dSPNs) and indirect-pathway (iSPNs); SNc dopaminergic units provide modulatory signal. Receptor-level detail is abstracted: D1/D2-like effect modelled by sign of plasticity parameter (α negative for dSPNs, positive for iSPNs) but no explicit biochemical cascades (no explicit DA receptor kinetics or DARPP-32 dynamics). |
| rpe_operationalization | RPE defined as a vector-valued instantaneous error per readout dimension: ϵ_k(t) = (r_k^SNr(t) − T_k(t)) * r_k^SNr(t) * (1 − r_k^SNr(t)). The model equates SNc firing rate r_k^SNc(t) with ϵ_k(t) (signed, can be negative as deviation from baseline). RPE is computed continuously (ms resolution) and used outcome-locked across the trial timecourse (loss ℓ(t) = 1/2 Σ_k (r_k^SNr(t) − T_k(t))^2). |
| dopamine_measurement_method | In-model computed dopamine concentration C_j(t) at each SPN: C_j(t) = Σ_k r_k^SNc(t) * d_jk, where d_jk is the time-invariant effective nigrostriatal weight arising from spatially distributed varicosities with exponential distance decay; RPE magnitude corresponds to SNc unit firing rates r_k^SNc(t) and local concentration C_j(t) (arbitrary model firing-rate units). |
| rpe_magnitude_metric | Metrics used in simulations: SNc firing rate r_k^SNc(t) (unitless firing-rate variable bounded via sigmoid transfer), and local dopamine concentration C_j(t) (linear sum of r_k^SNc * spatial kernel d_jk). Reported effects are summarized via network loss (L2 error) trajectories and final squared error after 5,000 trials (medians and quartiles across 25 runs); no physical units (model units, ms time bins). |
| learning_rate_metric | Rate of associative learning operationalized as reduction in task loss (L2 squared error between readout and target) across trials — measured as convergence speed of loss over 5,000 trials and final squared error after 5,000 trials; comparisons sometimes illustrated by trial-by-trial median loss and boxplots (median, quartiles, min/max across 25 runs). No fitted RL 'alpha' behavioral parameter estimated from behavior, but plasticity step sizes (α for corticostriatal, β for striatofugal) are fixed model parameters (α = ±2.5e−2, β = 1e−3). |
| relationship_direction_and_effect_size | Direction: heterogeneous, neuron-specific (vector-valued) dopamine feedback increases learning speed and reduces final L2 error relative to homogeneous (scalar) dopamine or no striatal plasticity. Effect size is reported qualitatively via median loss curves and boxplots: heterogeneous model shows faster decline and lower median squared error at trial 5,000 than homogeneous or no-striatal-plasticity controls; exact numeric statistics (e.g., p-values, effect-size coefficients) are not provided, only medians/quartiles across 25 simulations. |
| relationship_shape_nonlinearity | Nonlinear, nonmonotonic dependence on dopamine spatial spread: performance as a function of dopamine spatial constant λ shows a 'sweet spot' (intermediate λ) where feedback matrix D is sufficiently independent and learning is maximal; extremes (λ ≪ 1 or λ ≫ 1) produce poor performance (no feedback or effectively homogeneous feedback). Dependence on temporal dynamics τ_DA: slow low-pass filtering of dopamine degrades learning (monotonic deterioration with increasing τ_DA) unless synaptic high-pass compensation is included. Thus shape: inverted-U across spatial scale (λ) and monotonic decay across increasing τ_DA unless compensated. |
| valence_asymmetry | Model includes pathway-specific sign of plasticity (α negative for direct-pathway SPNs, positive for indirect-pathway SPNs) reflecting D1/D2 oppositional effects, but the simulations do not report separate quantitative learning-rate parameters for positive vs negative RPEs; negative ϵ_k(t) is handled as deviations from baseline, but no empirical test of asymmetric learning rates (α+ vs α−) is reported. |
| causal_manipulations_of_dopamine | In-silico causal manipulations: (i) vary spatial diffusion constant λ of dopamine (exponential distance decay) to alter mixing and independence of per-SPN feedback; (ii) vary number of varicosities per SNc neuron N_var; (iii) replace heterogeneous feedback with homogeneous (all SPNs receive same DA) or random/ideal feedback matrices; (iv) apply low-pass temporal filtering with time constant τ_DA to simulate slow dopamine dynamics; (v) remove/disable plasticity in striatofugal or corticostriatal projections (lesion-like manipulations). These causal manipulations change RPE distribution and demonstrate corresponding effects on learning (loss trajectories). No biological optogenetic/pharmacological manipulations were performed in vivo in this paper (an optogenetic experiment is proposed in Discussion). |
| plasticity_readouts | Plasticity is reported as change in model synaptic weights (corticostriatal p_ji eligibility traces × γ_j where γ_j = Σ_k ϵ_k(t) * d_jk) and adaptation of striatofugal weights w_kj^Str→SNr (driven by −β ϵ_k r_j^Str). Readouts are changes in firing rate dynamics and improved matching of SNr readout to targets. No physiological LTP/LTD (ms/Hz amplitude) or in vitro protocols are reported; plasticity effects are quantified via improved task loss and final weight alignment (feedback alignment d_jk ≈ learned w_kj^Str→SNr). |
| computational_model_details | Learning framework: local three-factor RFLO (random-feedback local online) learning in a recurrent rate network; loss ℓ(t)=1/2 Σ_k (r_k^SNr−T_k)^2; corticostriatal update dw_ji/dt = −α γ_j(t) p_ji(t) with p_ji an eligibility trace (τ_Str dp_ji/dt = −p_ji + r_j^Str(1−r_j^Str) r_i^Ctx). SNc encodes vector error ϵ_k(t) and dopamine diffusion produces C_j(t)=Σ_k r_k^SNc d_jk; striatofugal plasticity dw_kj^Str→SNr/dt = −β ϵ_k(t) r_j^Str. Key parameters: τ_m=10 ms, τ_task=20 ms (target timescale), α=±2.5e−2 (sign depends on pathway), β=1e−3, λ spatial decay default 0.1 (units: fraction of cube side), N_var = 10 by default. The model tests ideal feedback (D = W^T), random shuffled D, and dopamine-derived D (from varicosity positions). |
| contextual_modulators_and_confounds | Discussed moderators/confounds: spatial mixing of DA (λ) and varicosity density (N_var) determine independence/degeneracy of feedback matrix D; temporal filtering of dopamine (τ_DA) can impair learning unless synapses implement high-pass filtering; movement-related and non-RPE tuning of SNc units could confound interpretation of dopaminergic signals (movement vs error); assumptions omitted such as saturating DA receptors, cholinergic regulation of DA release, axonal release patterns; requirement that downstream readout projection be plastic for feedback alignment to work; noise and signal-to-noise constraints implicit in biochemical cascades not modeled explicitly. |
| boundary_conditions_and_negative_results | Boundary/negative conditions reported: when λ ≪ 1 (dopamine too local) almost no DA reaches many SPNs and model reverts to no-feedback control; when λ ≫ 1 (DA covers entire cube) DA is homogeneous and confers no benefit beyond readout-only plasticity; slow dopamine dynamics (large τ_DA) degrade learning performance and can make performance worse than no-striatal-plasticity controls unless synaptic high-pass filtering is implemented; disabling striatofugal plasticity abolishes the advantage of heterogeneous DA (feedback alignment cannot occur). |
| key_quoted_conclusions | 1) "Strikingly, [the homogeneous dopamine control] performed no better than the reduced model that only has learning in the readout layer." 2) "In between these two extremes [of dopamine spatial scale], where the dopamine scale is intermediate, there is a sweet spot where each SPN receives dopamine corresponding to a unique random linear projection of the 4-dimensional error... In this regime, the benefit of the feedback is the largest." 3) "We demonstrate that a heterogeneous, vector-valued feedback signal could in fact be transmitted by dopamine, even if the dopaminergic projections in the striatum are random." |
